original_bundle.answer: Yes, you can — but not in the way you're thinking. Your body is already working hard: 438 minutes of sleep, about 71% consistency, and a 46-minute strength session with a 15.4 load. The decision point is whether you want to keep the load where it is and keep the sleep, or move the load to a different day. Your HRV is 58 ms, right on your 30-day average of 56 ms, and resting heart rate is 55 bpm, close to your usual 56, so there's no warning sign of overtraining. The deciding factor is whether today is genuinely your hard day or just a normal one with a big session. If it's the latter, keep the load but cut the volume: keep the same number of sessions but drop the intensity, or swap a moderate session for a lighter one. If it's the former, keep the load as is and enjoy the extra sleep.

original_bundle.answer: Right now it means your body is absorbing training well — yesterday's 11.4 load was comfortably handled, and your 7-day recovery average of 65% is sitting above your 30-day baseline of 67%. The caveat is that it's a manual log, so sleep is the only measured signal, and last night's 444 minutes of sleep came with a debt of 11 minutes, which is a work day's worth. That's a normal night, not a bad one. The good news is your HRV of 57 ms is running below your 30-day average of 60 ms, so strain is being absorbed rather than stored — a suppressed HRV is the pattern to watch as the week progresses. I'd keep the same training plan, but expect a slightly lighter day on the hardest sessions.
